According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

{{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}, {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}} | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏}}

== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏}}

== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

{{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}, {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}} | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

{{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}, {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}} | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}

{{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}, {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}} | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}

{{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}, {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}} | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}

{{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}, {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}} | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}

== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

{{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}, {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}} | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

{{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}, {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}} | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>

== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|12=draft}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=draft}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶|12=draft}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|12=draft}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇|12=draft}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|12=draft}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏|1=Ō clan|12=draft}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=draft}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡|12=draft}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|12=draft}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=draft}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|12=draft}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺|12=draft}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=draft}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|12=draft}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan|12=draft}}

'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|qq=}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa City History|qq=}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwahime|qq=}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jitō|qq=}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏|1=Ō clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō clan|qq=}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|qq=}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa District|qq=}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiisagata District|qq=}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|qq=}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|qq=}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>

This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏|1=Ō clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō clan|qq=}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|qq=}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa District|qq=}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiisagata District|qq=}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|qq=}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|qq=}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|qq=}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkō-ji|qq=}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa-taisha|qq=}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Era|qq=}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|qq=}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}}

{{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province|qq=}}, {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiisagata District|qq=}}, {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa District|qq=}} | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Morizumi|qq=}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|qq=}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa City History|qq=}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|ja|太安万侶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwahime|qq=}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} during Empress {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jitō|qq=}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Omi Clan|ja|多氏|1=Ō clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō clan|qq=}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|qq=}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa District|qq=}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiisagata District|qq=}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|qq=}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|qq=}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|qq=}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|ja|善光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkō-ji|qq=}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa-taisha|qq=}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Jōgan Era|ja|貞観|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Era|qq=}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|qq=}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|qq=}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]]. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|qq=}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}} and was recognized for suppressing the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]] in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|qq=}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkō-ji]], basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan Era]], {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|qq=}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

{{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]], {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]] | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Morizumi|qq=}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|qq=}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], only {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|1=Takeminakata|2=ja|3=タケミナカタ|lt=Takeminakata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa City History|qq=}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, influenced [[:en:Ō no Yasumaro|Ō no Yasumaro]]—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwahime|qq=}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] during Empress [[:en:Empress Jitō|Jitō]]'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]], they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|qq=}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]]. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|qq=}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}} and was recognized for suppressing the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]] in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|qq=}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkō-ji]], basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan Era]], {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|qq=}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

{{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]], {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]] | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Morizumi|qq=}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], only [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]] appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa City History|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, influenced [[:en:Ō no Yasumaro|Ō no Yasumaro]]—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] during Empress [[:en:Empress Jitō|Jitō]]'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]], they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|qq=}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]]. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|qq=}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|qq=}} and was recognized for suppressing the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]] in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|qq=}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkō-ji]], basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan Era]], {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大貞長}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]]
