Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]]. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|1=kanasashinotoneri no yatsumaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98734335}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} and was recognized for suppressing the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]] in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|1=Minochi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548296}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkō-ji]], basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan Era]], {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大貞長|1=Ō no Sadanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711368}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金刺部氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]], {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]] | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Morizumi|1=Kana Moritomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11646497}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[:en:Kinmei|Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], only [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]] appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa City History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, influenced [[:en:Ō no Yasumaro|Ō no Yasumaro]]—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] during Empress [[:en:Empress Jitō|Jitō]]'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]], they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|1=Tadera Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377638}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]]. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|1=kanasashinotoneri no yatsumaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98734335}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} and was recognized for suppressing the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]] in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|1=Minochi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548296}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkō-ji]], basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan Era]], {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大貞長|1=Ō no Sadanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711368}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan Era]], {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大貞長|1=Ō no Sadanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711368}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金刺部氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]], {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]] | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Morizumi|1=Kana Moritomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11646497}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[:en:Kinmei|Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], only [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]] appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa City History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, influenced [[:en:Ō no Yasumaro|Ō no Yasumaro]]—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] during Empress [[:en:Empress Jitō|Jitō]]'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]], they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|1=Tadera Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377638}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]]. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|1=kanasashinotoneri no yatsumaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98734335}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} and was recognized for suppressing the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]] in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|1=Minochi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548296}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkō-ji]], basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan Era]], {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大貞長|1=Ō no Sadanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711368}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金刺部氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]], {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]] | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Morizumi|1=Kana Moritomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11646497}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[:en:Kinmei|Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], only [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]] appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa City History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, influenced [[:en:Ō no Yasumaro|Ō no Yasumaro]]—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] during Empress [[:en:Empress Jitō|Jitō]]'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]], they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|1=Tadera Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377638}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]]. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|1=kanasashinotoneri no yatsumaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98734335}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} and was recognized for suppressing the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]] in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|1=Minochi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548296}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkō-ji]], basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan Era]], {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大貞長|1=Ō no Sadanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711368}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[:en:Kinmei|Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], only [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]] appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa City History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, influenced [[:en:Ō no Yasumaro|Ō no Yasumaro]]—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] during Empress [[:en:Empress Jitō|Jitō]]'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]], they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|1=Tadera Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377638}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}}, [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and [[:en:Chiisagata District, Nagano|Chiisagata District]]. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|1=kanasashinotoneri no yatsumaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98734335}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} and was recognized for suppressing the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]] in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|1=Minochi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548296}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkō-ji]], basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan Era]], {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大貞長|1=Ō no Sadanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711368}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]]

'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[:en:Kinmei|Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa City History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|en|Ō no Yasumaro|WD=Q384705|lt=Ō no Yasumaro}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} during Empress {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|WD=Q232026|lt=Jitō}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|WD=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|1=Tadera Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377638}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}}, {{ill|Suwa District, Nagano|en|Suwa District, Nagano|WD=Q1131473|lt=Suwa District}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District, Nagano|en|Chiisagata District, Nagano|WD=Q1072595|lt=Chiisagata District}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|1=kanasashinotoneri no yatsumaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98734335}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|1=Minochi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548296}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|WD=Q189640|lt=Zenkō-ji}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan Era}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大貞長|1=Ō no Sadanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711368}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan}}

== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[:en:Kinmei|Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa City History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|en|Ō no Yasumaro|WD=Q384705|lt=Ō no Yasumaro}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} during Empress {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|WD=Q232026|lt=Jitō}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|WD=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|1=Tadera Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377638}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}}, {{ill|Suwa District, Nagano|en|Suwa District, Nagano|WD=Q1131473|lt=Suwa District}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District, Nagano|en|Chiisagata District, Nagano|WD=Q1072595|lt=Chiisagata District}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|1=kanasashinotoneri no yatsumaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98734335}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|1=Minochi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548296}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|WD=Q189640|lt=Zenkō-ji}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan Era}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大貞長|1=Ō no Sadanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711368}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}

'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[:en:Kinmei|Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|1=Takeminakata}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa City History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|en|Ō no Yasumaro|WD=Q384705|lt=Ō no Yasumaro|1=Ō no Yasumaro}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} during Empress {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|WD=Q232026|lt=Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|WD=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan|1=Ō clan}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|1=Tadera Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377638}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}}, {{ill|Suwa District, Nagano|en|Suwa District, Nagano|WD=Q1131473|lt=Suwa District|1=Suwa District, Nagano}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District, Nagano|en|Chiisagata District, Nagano|WD=Q1072595|lt=Chiisagata District|1=Chiisagata District, Nagano}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|1=kanasashinotoneri no yatsumaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98734335}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|1=Minochi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548296}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|WD=Q189640|lt=Zenkō-ji|1=Zenkō-ji}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa Taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan Era|1=Zhenguan}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大貞長|1=Ō no Sadanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711368}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.

{{Infobox Ancient Japanese Clan | clan_name = Kanasashi Clan (金刺氏) | image = | clan_surname = Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] | founder = [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]] | lineage = [[Omi Clan]] | progenitor = [[Take Iyakabe no Mikoto]] | classification = [[Imperial Descent]] | main_territory = {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|qid=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, {{ill|Chiisagata District, Nagano|en|Chiisagata District, Nagano|qid=Q1072595|lt=Chiisagata District|1=Chiisagata District, Nagano}}, {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11380911}}, {{ill|Suwa District, Nagano|en|Suwa District, Nagano|qid=Q1131473|lt=Suwa District|1=Suwa District, Nagano}} | notable_figures = {{ill|Kanasashi Morizumi|ja|金刺盛澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Morizumi|1=Kana Moritomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11646497}} | descendants = }}
'''Kanasashi Clan''' (金刺氏) was one of the ancient Japanese clans.
== Overview == The Kanasashi Clan is believed to have taken their name from serving as [[Toneribe]] retainers under Emperor [[:en:Kinmei|Kinmei]] during the relocation of the court to {{ill|Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja|磯城嶋金刺宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikishima-Kanasashi Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="#1">Sato, Yuichi, ''The Clans and Beliefs of Ancient Shinano'' (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021)</ref>
In The {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, only {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|qid=Q9150391|qid=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|1=Takeminakata}} appears in the legend of the land transfer. According to the {{ill|Suwa City History|ja|諏訪市史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa City History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Kanasashi Clan, descendants of the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|en|Ō no Yasumaro|qid=Q384705|lt=Ō no Yasumaro|1=Ō no Yasumaro}}—who shared the same lineage through their progenitor, [[Kamuyai Mimi no Mikoto]]—to insert Takeminakata's myth.<ref>Suwa City History Editorial Committee, ''Suwa City History: Volume 1'' (Suwa City, 1995)</ref>
It is hypothesized that the "fabricated deity" Takeminakata replaced Suwa's original deity, {{ill|Suwahime|ja|須波神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who was a water deity mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} during Empress {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|qid=Q232026|lt=Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}}'s reign. This shift occurred because the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan, who had served Emperor Kinmei, gained control over the Suwa region in the latter half of the 6th century. They collaborated with the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏|1=Moriya clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriya clan|qq=}} to conduct rituals, raising their status. This connection is reflected in Takeminakata's promotion within the pantheon.<ref name="#1"/> Furthermore, being closely related to the {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|qid=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan|1=Ō clan}}, they allegedly influenced {{ill|Ō no Yasumaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō no Yasumaro|qq=}} to incorporate Takeminakata's myth into the ''Kojiki''.<ref name="#1"/>
From the [[Nara Period]] to the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], Shinano's local administration was led by the Kanasashi [[Toneribe]] Clan and {{ill|Tadera Clan|ja|他田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadera Clan|1=Tadera Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11377638}}. Members of the Kanasashi Clan were prominent as local governors in {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11380911}}, {{ill|Suwa District, Nagano|en|Suwa District, Nagano|qid=Q1131473|lt=Suwa District|1=Suwa District, Nagano}}, and {{ill|Chiisagata District, Nagano|en|Chiisagata District, Nagano|qid=Q1072595|lt=Chiisagata District|1=Chiisagata District, Nagano}}. One key figure was {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Hachiro|1=kanasashinotoneri no yatsumaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q98734335}}, who served as governor of {{ill|Inagōri District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inagōri District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11380911}} and was recognized for suppressing the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|qid=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}} in 764.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Volume 25, Emperor Shōtoku, 764 AD</ref>
The Kanasashi Clan maintained strong ties to central authorities through their role in horse breeding, which was essential for both transportation and military purposes. Five of the 16 imperial horse pastures in Shinano Province were located in the southern region.<ref name="#1"/>
== Religious Influence == A branch of the Kanasashi Clan in {{ill|Mizuno District|ja|水内郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno District|1=Minochi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11548296}} is said to have contributed to the establishment of {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|qid=Q189640|lt=Zenkō-ji|1=Zenkō-ji}}, basing themselves in what is now Nagano City.<ref>Kiriwara, Ken, ''My Notes on Ancient Studies'' (Shinmai Book Center, 1983)</ref>
== Forged Lineages in the "Genealogy of Kami" == The "Genealogy of Kami," kept by the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|qid=Q218813|lt=Suwa Taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} Shrine, claims that the Kanasashi Clan descended from the {{ill|Shinano Province Clan Leader|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province Clan Leader|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. However, these genealogies were fabricated during the early modern period to legitimize the clan's identity and connection to the shrine.<ref>Maeda, Ryotaro, "Reconsidering the Genealogies of Suwa Taisha," ''Journal of Japanese Literature and Culture,'' Issue 161 (Hokkaido University, August 2023)</ref>
== Legacy == In the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|qid=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan Era|1=Zhenguan}}, {{ill|Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja|金刺舎人貞長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanasashi Toneri Sadamitsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大貞長|1=Ō no Sadanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q105711368}}, a member of the Kanasashi Clan, was promoted to various positions within the imperial court but later shifted to a religious role within the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|qq=}}. This transition illustrates the gradual decline of the clan's political influence in favor of religious and cultural pursuits.
